terms.definition: A simple CVC word: C + A + T. C says /k/ before A (Rule 1). Closed syllable keeps A short.
terms.exampleUsage: The CAT sat on the mat.

terms.definition: A CVC word: B + E + D. Closed syllable — E says short /e/. The consonants close the syllable keeping E short.
terms.exampleUsage: I made my BED this morning.

terms.definition: A CVC word: S + I + T. Closed syllable — I says short /i/.
terms.exampleUsage: Please SIT down.

terms.definition: A CVC word: H + O + T. Closed syllable — O says short /o/.
terms.exampleUsage: The sun is HOT today.

terms.definition: A CVC word: C + U + P. C says /k/ before U (Rule 1 — C only softens before E, I, Y).
terms.exampleUsage: Pour water in the CUP.

terms.definition: Uses the SH consonant team: SH + I + P. SH makes one sound /sh/, not /s/ + /h/.
terms.exampleUsage: The SHIP sailed across the ocean.

terms.definition: Uses the TH consonant team: TH + A + T. TH says voiced /th/ here (vocal cords vibrate).
terms.exampleUsage: Look at THAT bird!

terms.definition: Uses the NG consonant team: R + I + NG. NG makes a single nasal sound /ng/ at the back of the throat.
terms.exampleUsage: She wore a gold RING.

terms.definition: Uses the CK shield: D + U + CK. CK is used after a single short vowel (Rule 6). The C-K pair protects the short U.
terms.exampleUsage: The DUCK swam in the pond.

terms.definition: Uses the CK shield: B + A + CK. CK protects the short A sound (Rule 6).
terms.exampleUsage: Come BACK soon!

terms.definition: Uses the AI vowel team: R + AI + N. AI is used in the middle of words for /ay/ (Rule 9).
terms.exampleUsage: The RAIN fell all day.

terms.definition: Uses the AY vowel team: P + L + AY. AY is used at the end of base words for /ay/ (Rule 9).
terms.exampleUsage: Let's go PLAY outside!

terms.definition: Uses the EE vowel team: T + R + EE. EE always says /ee/ — one of the most reliable vowel teams.
terms.exampleUsage: The TREE has green leaves.

terms.definition: Uses the OA vowel team: G + OA + T. OA says long O /oh/ — very reliable.
terms.exampleUsage: The GOAT ate the grass.

terms.definition: Uses the OI vowel team: C + OI + N. OI is used in the middle of words for /oy/ (Rule 10).
terms.exampleUsage: I found a gold COIN.

terms.definition: Uses the OY vowel team: B + OY. OY is used at the end of words for /oy/ (Rule 10).
terms.exampleUsage: The BOY ran fast.

terms.definition: Uses the OW vowel team: C + OW. OW says /ow/ here (can also say /oh/ as in SNOW).
terms.exampleUsage: The COW gave us milk.

terms.definition: Uses the AR R-controlled vowel: C + AR. R takes control of A and changes its sound to /ar/.
terms.exampleUsage: We drove the CAR to school.

terms.definition: Uses the IR R-controlled vowel: B + IR + D. IR sounds exactly like ER and UR — all three say /er/.
terms.exampleUsage: The BIRD sang a beautiful song.

terms.definition: Uses the OR R-controlled vowel: F + OR + K. R controls the O to produce the /or/ sound.
terms.exampleUsage: Eat with a FORK and knife.

terms.definition: Y acts as a vowel: F + L + Y. In one-syllable words, Y says /eye/ (Rule 6).
terms.exampleUsage: Watch the birds FLY high.

terms.definition: Uses the OO vowel team: F + OO + D. OO says long /oo/ as in moon.
terms.exampleUsage: The FOOD was delicious.

terms.definition: Uses the OO vowel team: B + OO + K. OO says short /oo/ here — different from FOOD.
terms.exampleUsage: I read a BOOK every night.

terms.definition: Uses the QU team: QU + I + T. Q always appears with U, saying /kw/ (Rule 11).
terms.exampleUsage: Don't QUIT trying!

terms.definition: O before LD: C + O + L + D. O says long /oh/ before LD — a common pattern.
terms.exampleUsage: It is very COLD outside.

terms.definition: I before ND: K + I + N + D. I says long /eye/ before ND — the consonant cluster keeps I long.
terms.exampleUsage: She was very KIND to everyone.

terms.definition: Uses the GN consonant team: GN + A + T. G is silent before N (Rule 33).
terms.exampleUsage: A GNAT buzzed around my head.

terms.definition: Silent E makes A long: SH + A + K + E. Silent E Job 1 — the vowel says its name (Rule 17).
terms.exampleUsage: SHAKE the bottle before opening.

terms.definition: Greek PH + Silent E: PH + O + N + E. PH says /f/ (Greek origin). Silent E makes O long (Rule 17).
terms.exampleUsage: Answer the PHONE quickly.

terms.definition: Two silent letters: WR + I + T + E. W is silent in WR (Rule 33). Silent E makes I long (Rule 17).
terms.exampleUsage: Please WRITE your name here.

terms.definition: Silent K + Silent E: KN + I + F + E. K is silent in KN (Rule 33). Silent E makes I long (Rule 17).
terms.exampleUsage: Use a KNIFE to cut the bread.

terms.definition: Silent E guards V: G + L + O + V + E. English words don't end in V (Rule 3). This is Silent E Job 2, not long O.
terms.exampleUsage: Put on your GLOVE before going out.

terms.definition: Silent E keeps C soft: D + A + N + C + E. Without E, the C would say /k/. This is Silent E Job 3 (Rule 19).
terms.exampleUsage: Let's DANCE to the music!

terms.definition: DGE shield: B + A + DGE. D protects the short A before the /j/ sound (Rule 8).
terms.exampleUsage: She wore a BADGE on her shirt.

terms.definition: TCH shield: M + A + TCH. T protects the short A before the /ch/ sound (Rule 7).
terms.exampleUsage: Light the MATCH carefully.

terms.definition: IGH pattern: N + IGH + T. IGH always says /eye/ with GH silent (Rule 37). One of English's most reliable patterns.
terms.exampleUsage: Good NIGHT! Sleep well.

terms.definition: IGH pattern: L + IGH + T. Three letters (IGH) making one sound /eye/.
terms.exampleUsage: Turn on the LIGHT.

terms.definition: UE vowel team: B + L + UE. E guards U since English words don't end in U alone (Rule 3/18).
terms.exampleUsage: The sky is BLUE today.

terms.definition: GH at start + long O: GH + O + S + T. GH says /g/ at the start of words. O is long before ST.
terms.exampleUsage: A GHOST appeared at midnight!

terms.definition: Double F after short vowel: C + L + I + FF. In one-syllable words, final /f/ doubles after a short vowel (Rule 5).
terms.exampleUsage: Be careful near the CLIFF edge!

terms.definition: Doubled consonant before suffix: R + U + N + N + I + NG. Run → running: double N before vowel suffix to keep U short (Rule 14).
terms.exampleUsage: She is RUNNING in the park.

terms.definition: Drop E before vowel suffix: H + O + P + ED. Hope → hoped: silent E dropped before -ED (Rule 12). ED says /t/ after unvoiced /p/.
terms.exampleUsage: I HOPED for a sunny day.

terms.definition: ED says /ed/ after T: W + A + N + T + ED. When the base ends in T or D, -ED adds a whole new syllable /ed/ (Rule 22).
terms.exampleUsage: She WANTED a new book.

terms.definition: ED says /t/ after unvoiced P: J + U + M + P + ED. After unvoiced consonants, -ED says /t/ (Rule 22).
terms.exampleUsage: The frog JUMPED into the pond.

terms.definition: ED says /d/ after voiced L: C + A + LL + ED. After voiced consonants, -ED says /d/ (Rule 22).
terms.exampleUsage: Mom CALLED me for dinner.

terms.definition: Add -ES after hissing X: F + O + X + ES. Words ending in S, X, Z, CH, SH need -ES, not just -S (Rule 23).
terms.exampleUsage: Two FOXES ran through the forest.

terms.definition: Double consonant before suffix: B + I + G + G + ER. Big → bigger: double G before vowel suffix (Rule 14) to keep I short.
terms.exampleUsage: This house is BIGGER than that one.

terms.definition: AI vowel team in middle: T + R + AI + N. AI says /ay/ in the middle of words (Rule 9).
terms.exampleUsage: The TRAIN arrived on time.

terms.definition: EA vowel team: C + R + EA + M. EA most commonly says /ee/ (long E).
terms.exampleUsage: I love ice CREAM!

terms.definition: UI vowel team: F + R + UI + T. UI says /oo/ here — a less common vowel team.
terms.exampleUsage: Eat plenty of FRUIT every day.

terms.definition: Y changes to I: H + A + PP + I + ER. Happy → happier: consonant + Y changes to I before -ER suffix (Rule 15).
terms.exampleUsage: Today I am HAPPIER than yesterday.

terms.definition: Y to I before -ED: C + AR + R + I + ED. Carry → carried: consonant + Y changes to I before -ED (Rule 15).
terms.exampleUsage: She CARRIED the heavy bag.

terms.definition: Vowel + Y stays: S + T + AY + I + NG. Stay → staying: vowel + Y keeps the Y (Rule 16).
terms.exampleUsage: We are STAYING home today.

terms.definition: The -FUL suffix: C + AR + E + F + U + L. FULL becomes -FUL as a suffix, dropping one L (Rule 27).
terms.exampleUsage: Be CAREFUL crossing the road.

terms.definition: Keep E before consonant suffix: H + O + P + E + FUL. Hope → hopeful: keep silent E before -FUL (consonant suffix, Rule 13).
terms.exampleUsage: I am HOPEFUL about tomorrow.

terms.definition: OUGH combo: TH + OUGH + T. OUGH says /aw/ here — one of its six possible sounds.
terms.exampleUsage: I THOUGHT about it carefully.

terms.definition: OUGH combo: TH + R + OUGH. OUGH says /oo/ here — a different sound from THOUGHT.
terms.exampleUsage: Walk THROUGH the door.

terms.definition: AUGH combo: D + AUGH + T + ER. AUGH says /aw/ — four letters for one sound. GH is silent.
terms.exampleUsage: My DAUGHTER loves to read.

terms.definition: AUGH combo: C + AUGH + T. AUGH always says /aw/ — a reliable pattern despite its length.
terms.exampleUsage: He CAUGHT the ball!

terms.definition: EIGH combo: F + R + EIGH + T. EIGH says /ay/ — four letters for one sound. GH is silent.
terms.exampleUsage: The FREIGHT train carried goods.

terms.definition: Two silent patterns: KN + IGH + T. K is silent in KN (Rule 33). GH is silent in IGH (Rule 37).
terms.exampleUsage: The brave KNIGHT rode into battle.

terms.definition: TI special phonogram: N + A + TI + O + N. TI says /sh/ at the start of the -TION suffix (Rule 25).
terms.exampleUsage: Our NATION celebrated its independence.

terms.definition: CI special phonogram: S + P + E + CI + A + L. CI says /sh/ at the start of the -CIAL suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: This is a SPECIAL day!

terms.definition: SI special phonogram: V + I + SI + O + N. SI says /zh/ here — the voiced version of /sh/.
terms.exampleUsage: She had a clear VISION of the future.

terms.definition: Multiple patterns: QU + I + CK + L + Y. QU team /kw/ + CK shield after short I + Y says /ee/ in multi-syllable words.
terms.exampleUsage: Run QUICKLY to catch the bus!

terms.definition: Compound word with IGH: MID + NIGHT. Two words combined, with the IGH pattern saying /eye/ in NIGHT.
terms.exampleUsage: The clock struck MIDNIGHT.

terms.definition: AL prefix from ALL: AL + READY. When ALL is a prefix, it drops one L — becomes AL- (Rule 27).
terms.exampleUsage: I have ALREADY finished my homework.

terms.definition: ES after hissing TCH: W + A + TCH + ES. Add -ES (not just -S) after TCH because it hisses (Rule 23).
terms.exampleUsage: She WATCHES the sunset every evening.

terms.definition: Schwa vowel: B + A + LL + OO + N. The first A is unstressed and becomes a schwa /uh/ sound.
terms.exampleUsage: The red BALLOON floated away.

terms.definition: Greek PH: E + L + E + PH + A + N + T. PH says /f/ — from Greek (Rule 38). Second E is schwa.
terms.exampleUsage: The ELEPHANT is the largest land animal.

terms.definition: UI vowel team: B + UI + L + D + I + NG. UI unusually says short /i/ in BUILD — an irregular pattern.
terms.exampleUsage: They are BUILDING a new school.

terms.definition: Silent W + NK team: WR + I + NK + L + E. W is silent in WR (Rule 33). NK says /ngk/. Consonant-LE ending.
terms.exampleUsage: Iron the WRINKLE out of your shirt.

terms.definition: UE guards U: C + O + N + T + I + N + UE. English words don't end in U alone, so E follows (Rule 3).
terms.exampleUsage: Please CONTINUE reading the story.

terms.definition: UE guards U: R + E + S + C + UE. E follows U because English words cannot end in U (Rule 3).
terms.exampleUsage: The firefighters came to RESCUE the cat.

terms.definition: UI vowel team: J + UI + C + E. UI says /oo/ here. Soft C before E.
terms.exampleUsage: Would you like some orange JUICE?

terms.definition: NK + EY: D + O + NK + EY. NK says /ngk/. EY says /ee/ at the end of multi-syllable words.
terms.exampleUsage: The DONKEY carried the heavy load.

terms.definition: AL prefix + OUGH: AL + TH + OUGH. AL from ALL (drops one L). OUGH says /oh/ here.
terms.exampleUsage: ALTHOUGH it rained, we had fun.

terms.definition: Silent E does two jobs: S + T + A + G + E. E makes A long /ay/ (Job 1) AND keeps G soft /j/ (Job 3).
terms.exampleUsage: The actors performed on STAGE.

terms.definition: EIGH combo: S + L + EIGH. EIGH says /ay/ — four letters for one sound.
terms.exampleUsage: Santa rides a SLEIGH with reindeer.

terms.definition: Double S after short vowel: G + R + A + SS. SS after short A in one-syllable word (Rule 5).
terms.exampleUsage: The GRASS is green after the rain.

terms.definition: Double L after short vowel: S + P + E + LL. LL after short E in one-syllable word (Rule 5).
terms.exampleUsage: Can you SPELL your name?

terms.definition: Double F after short vowel: S + T + U + FF. FF after short U in one-syllable word (Rule 5).
terms.exampleUsage: Put your STUFF in the bag.

terms.definition: Double S after short vowel: D + R + E + SS. SS after short E in one-syllable word (Rule 5).
terms.exampleUsage: She wore a beautiful DRESS.

terms.definition: OA vowel team: F + L + OA + T. OA reliably says long O /oh/.
terms.exampleUsage: Boats FLOAT on water.

terms.definition: OI vowel team: P + OI + N + T. OI says /oy/ in the middle (Rule 10).
terms.exampleUsage: She made a good POINT.

terms.definition: SH team + OU team: SH + OU + T. Two teams in one word — SH says /sh/ and OU says /ow/.
terms.exampleUsage: Don't SHOUT in the library.

terms.definition: AU vowel team: H + AU + N + T. AU says /aw/ in the middle of words.
terms.exampleUsage: Ghosts HAUNT the old castle.

terms.definition: IR R-controlled: S + T + IR. IR says /er/ — same sound as ER and UR.
terms.exampleUsage: STIR the soup with a spoon.

terms.definition: UR R-controlled: B + UR + N. UR says /er/ — the third /er/ triplet.
terms.exampleUsage: Don't BURN the toast!

terms.definition: ER R-controlled: H + ER. ER says /er/ — one of the /er/ triplets (ER, IR, UR).
terms.exampleUsage: Give HER the book.

terms.definition: Y to I before -ED: study → studied. Consonant + Y changes to I before -ED suffix (Rule 15).
terms.exampleUsage: He STUDIED hard for the test.

terms.definition: Y to I before -LY: happy → happily. Consonant + Y changes to I before -LY (Rule 15).
terms.exampleUsage: They lived HAPPILY ever after.

terms.definition: Double T before suffix: sit → sitting. Double T keeps I short before vowel suffix (Rule 14).
terms.exampleUsage: She was SITTING by the window.

terms.definition: Double P before suffix: hop → hopping. Double P keeps O short (Rule 14). Compare with HOPING (no double).
terms.exampleUsage: The rabbit was HOPPING around the garden.

terms.definition: ES after hissing SH: dish → dishes. Add -ES after SH because it hisses (Rule 23).
terms.exampleUsage: Please wash the DISHES after dinner.

terms.definition: ES after hissing X: box → boxes. Add -ES after X because /ks/ is a hissing sound (Rule 23).
terms.exampleUsage: Stack the BOXES in the corner.

terms.definition: SI special phonogram + SS: S + E + SS + I + O + N. SI says /sh/ in -SION. Note the double SS.
terms.exampleUsage: The study SESSION lasted two hours.

terms.definition: SI special phonogram: T + E + N + SI + O + N. SI says /sh/ in the -SION suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: There was TENSION in the room.

terms.definition: TI special phonogram: A + C + TI + O + N. TI says /sh/ in the -TION suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: It's time for ACTION!

terms.definition: CI special phonogram: S + O + CI + A + L. CI says /sh/ in the -CIAL suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: Humans are SOCIAL creatures.

terms.definition: AW vowel team: S + AW. AW says /aw/ — used at the end of words and before certain consonants.
terms.exampleUsage: I SAW a rainbow after the rain.

terms.definition: EW vowel team: N + EW. EW says /oo/ here.
terms.exampleUsage: I got a NEW pair of shoes.

terms.definition: Long O before ST: M + O + S + T. O says long /oh/ before ST — a common pattern.
terms.exampleUsage: She ate MOST of the cake.

terms.definition: Long I before ND: F + I + N + D. I says long /eye/ before ND.
terms.exampleUsage: Can you FIND my keys?

terms.definition: Long O before LD: G + O + L + D. O says long /oh/ before LD.
terms.exampleUsage: The ring was made of GOLD.

terms.definition: Y as long I: C + R + Y. In one-syllable words, Y says /eye/.
terms.exampleUsage: Don't CRY, everything will be okay.

terms.definition: Y as long I: S + K + Y. In one-syllable words ending in Y, it says /eye/.
terms.exampleUsage: Look at the beautiful blue SKY!

terms.definition: WH team + Y: WH + Y. WH consonant team + Y saying /eye/ as a one-syllable word.
terms.exampleUsage: WHY is the sky blue?

terms.definition: WH consonant team: WH + E + N. WH makes one sound at the start.
terms.exampleUsage: WHEN does the movie start?

terms.definition: NK consonant team: B + A + NK. NK says /ngk/ — the N nasalizes before K.
terms.exampleUsage: She put her money in the BANK.

terms.definition: SH at the end: F + I + SH. SH consonant team says /sh/ — one sound from two letters.
terms.exampleUsage: The FISH swam in the river.

terms.definition: TH at the end: M + A + TH. TH says unvoiced /th/ at the end — no vibration.
terms.exampleUsage: I love solving MATH problems.

terms.definition: CK shield: L + O + CK. CK after short O in one-syllable word (Rule 6).
terms.exampleUsage: LOCK the door when you leave.

terms.definition: CK shield: K + I + CK. CK after short I (Rule 6).
terms.exampleUsage: KICK the ball into the goal!

terms.definition: EE vowel team: S + EE + D. EE always says /ee/ — very reliable.
terms.exampleUsage: Plant the SEED in the soil.

terms.definition: OA vowel team: B + OA + T. OA says long O /oh/.
terms.exampleUsage: We sailed the BOAT across the lake.

terms.definition: EA vowel team: B + EA + N. EA says /ee/ here — its most common sound.
terms.exampleUsage: I ate a green BEAN for lunch.

terms.definition: AY vowel team: D + AY. AY at the end of a base word says /ay/ (Rule 9).
terms.exampleUsage: What a beautiful DAY!

terms.definition: OU vowel team: OU + T. OU says /ow/ here.
terms.exampleUsage: Let's go OUT to the park.

terms.definition: Silent E makes U long: F + L + U + T + E. Silent E Job 1 — U says /oo/ (Rule 17).
terms.exampleUsage: She plays the FLUTE beautifully.

terms.definition: Silent E makes A long: G + R + A + P + E. Silent E Job 1 — A says /ay/ (Rule 17).
terms.exampleUsage: I love eating purple GRAPES.

terms.definition: Silent E jumps over consonant: F + L + A + M + E. Silent E makes A say /ay/ even across M.
terms.exampleUsage: The candle FLAME flickered in the wind.

terms.definition: Silent E makes I long: P + R + I + Z + E. Silent E Job 1 — I says /eye/ (Rule 17).
terms.exampleUsage: She won first PRIZE!

terms.definition: E guards V + R-controlled: N + ER + V + E. E after V (Rule 18) + ER R-controlled vowel.
terms.exampleUsage: She had the NERVE to speak up.

terms.definition: E guards V: S + O + L + V + E. Silent E Job 2 — V can't end a word alone (Rule 18).
terms.exampleUsage: Can you SOLVE this puzzle?

terms.definition: E keeps C soft: F + E + N + C + E. Silent E Job 3 — keeps C saying /s/ (Rule 19).
terms.exampleUsage: The garden has a white FENCE.

terms.definition: WH team + Silent E: WH + A + L + E. WH consonant team + silent E makes A long.
terms.exampleUsage: The blue WHALE is the largest animal on Earth.

terms.definition: CH consonant team: CH + O + P. CH says /ch/ — the English pronunciation.
terms.exampleUsage: CHOP the vegetables for the salad.

terms.definition: TCH shield: D + I + TCH. TCH after short I protects the vowel (Rule 7).
terms.exampleUsage: There was a DITCH along the road.

terms.definition: DGE shield: F + U + DGE. DGE protects the short U before /j/ sound (Rule 8).
terms.exampleUsage: I love chocolate FUDGE!

terms.definition: DGE shield: L + E + DGE. DGE after short E for /j/ sound (Rule 8).
terms.exampleUsage: The bird sat on the window LEDGE.

terms.definition: TCH shield: N + O + TCH. TCH after short O (Rule 7).
terms.exampleUsage: Cut a NOTCH in the stick.

terms.definition: TCH shield: F + E + TCH. TCH protects the short E (Rule 7).
terms.exampleUsage: The dog will FETCH the ball.

terms.definition: TCH shield: C + A + TCH. TCH after short A for /ch/ (Rule 7).
terms.exampleUsage: CATCH the ball!

terms.definition: IGH pattern: S + IGH + T. IGH says /eye/ — GH is always silent in this pattern.
terms.exampleUsage: What a beautiful SIGHT!

terms.definition: IGH pattern: F + IGH + T. Three letters making one sound /eye/.
terms.exampleUsage: Don't FIGHT with your friends.

terms.definition: AUGH combo: N + AUGH + T + Y. AUGH says /aw/. Y says /ee/ in multi-syllable words.
terms.exampleUsage: The NAUGHTY puppy chewed the shoe.

terms.definition: EIGH + ED: W + EIGH + ED. EIGH says /ay/. ED says /d/ after voiced sound.
terms.exampleUsage: She WEIGHED the fruit at the market.

terms.definition: IGH + ER suffix: B + R + IGH + T + ER. IGH says /eye/. -ER comparative suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: The sun is BRIGHTER today.

terms.definition: Z at start: Z + I + G + Z + A + G. /z/ at start spelled with Z. Two syllables repeating a pattern.
terms.exampleUsage: The road goes in a ZIGZAG pattern.

terms.definition: Simple consonants: W + A + X. X says /ks/ at the end of words.
terms.exampleUsage: The candle is made of WAX.

terms.definition: Simple CVC: J + A + M. J always says /j/ — no exceptions.
terms.exampleUsage: I put JAM on my toast.

terms.definition: Simple CVC: V + A + N. V says /v/ — a reliable consonant.
terms.exampleUsage: The delivery VAN arrived.

terms.definition: Simple CVC: R + U + N. Closed syllable keeps U short.
terms.exampleUsage: Let's RUN to the park!

terms.definition: Simple CVC: F + I + G. Closed syllable keeps I short.
terms.exampleUsage: The FIG tree has sweet fruit.

terms.definition: Simple CVC: L + O + G. Closed syllable keeps O short.
terms.exampleUsage: Sit on the LOG by the campfire.

terms.definition: Simple CVC: P + E + N. Closed syllable keeps E short.
terms.exampleUsage: Write with a PEN.

terms.definition: Simple CVC: K + I + T. Closed syllable keeps I short.
terms.exampleUsage: Pack your first aid KIT.

terms.definition: Simple CVC: H + U + G. G says hard /g/ at the end.
terms.exampleUsage: Give your mom a HUG.

terms.definition: Y as long I: D + R + Y. In one-syllable words, Y says /eye/.
terms.exampleUsage: Let the clothes DRY in the sun.

terms.definition: TH team + -FUL suffix: truth + ful. TH consonant team + -FUL drops one L from FULL.
terms.exampleUsage: Always be TRUTHFUL.

terms.definition: TH + NK + -FUL: TH + A + NK + FUL. Two consonant teams (TH, NK) + -FUL suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: I am THANKFUL for my family.

terms.definition: AY team + -FUL suffix: play + ful. AY says /ay/ at end of base. -FUL drops one L.
terms.exampleUsage: The PLAYFUL kitten chased the ball.

terms.definition: Y to I before -EST: easy → easiest. Consonant + Y changes to I before -EST (Rule 15).
terms.exampleUsage: This was the EASIEST question.

terms.definition: Y to I before -ED: cry → cried. Y changes to I before -ED (Rule 15).
terms.exampleUsage: The baby CRIED all night.

terms.definition: Vowel + Y stays: OY + ED. OY at end of base keeps Y (Rule 16). ED says /d/.
terms.exampleUsage: We ENJOYED the movie.

terms.definition: AW team + -ING: draw + ing. AW vowel team says /aw/.
terms.exampleUsage: She is DRAWING a picture.

terms.definition: CH + EW + ING: chew + ing. CH consonant team + EW vowel team + -ING suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: The dog was CHEWING on a bone.

terms.definition: DGE + -ES plural: bridge + es. DGE after short I + -ES for plural.
terms.exampleUsage: The city has many BRIDGES.

terms.definition: TCH + ED: sketch + ed. TCH shield + ED suffix says /t/ after unvoiced sound.
terms.exampleUsage: She SKETCHED the landscape.

terms.definition: SH + -ES: brush + es. Add -ES after SH because it hisses (Rule 23).
terms.exampleUsage: She BRUSHES her hair every morning.

terms.definition: CK + -ER: track + er. CK after short A + -ER suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: The TRACKER followed the animal prints.

terms.definition: Silent G + -ING: sign + ing. G is silent in GN (Rule 33). I says long /eye/.
terms.exampleUsage: She is SIGNING the document.

terms.definition: GN team + AW team + -ING: gnaw + ing. G is silent in GN. AW says /aw/.
terms.exampleUsage: The mouse was GNAWING on the cheese.

terms.definition: IR + double R + -ING: stir + ring. IR R-controlled + double R before vowel suffix (Rule 14).
terms.exampleUsage: She was STIRRING the pot.

terms.definition: DGE + -ING: judge + ing. Drop E before vowel suffix (Rule 12). DGE shield preserved.
terms.exampleUsage: The panel is JUDGING the competition.

terms.definition: Silent E makes O long: S + P + O + K + E. Silent E Job 1 (Rule 17).
terms.exampleUsage: She SPOKE clearly to the audience.

terms.definition: Drop E before -ED: bake → baked. Silent E dropped before vowel suffix (Rule 12). ED says /t/.
terms.exampleUsage: Mom BAKED a chocolate cake.

terms.definition: Drop E before -ED: like → liked. Silent E dropped (Rule 12). ED says /t/ after unvoiced /k/.
terms.exampleUsage: Everyone LIKED the new song.

terms.definition: ED says /ed/ after D: need + ed. Base ends in D, so ED adds a syllable (Rule 22).
terms.exampleUsage: I NEEDED help with my homework.

terms.definition: Vowel + Y stays + ED: play + ed. AY keeps Y (Rule 16). ED says /d/ after voiced sound.
terms.exampleUsage: The children PLAYED in the garden.

terms.definition: Double N + -ER: run → runner. Double N before vowel suffix to keep U short (Rule 14).
terms.exampleUsage: The RUNNER crossed the finish line.

terms.definition: Double F after short vowel: S + T + I + FF. FF after short I in one-syllable word (Rule 5).
terms.exampleUsage: The cardboard was STIFF and hard to bend.

terms.definition: UR R-controlled + E guards V: C + UR + V + E. UR says /er/. E follows V (Rule 18).
terms.exampleUsage: The road has a sharp CURVE.

terms.definition: UR R-controlled: T + UR + N + I + P. UR says /er/ in the first syllable.
terms.exampleUsage: We grew a big TURNIP in the garden.

terms.definition: AI vowel team in unstressed position: C + A + P + T + AI + N. The AI loses its usual /ay/ in the unstressed syllable.
terms.exampleUsage: The CAPTAIN steered the ship.

terms.definition: AL prefix + AY team: AL + W + AY + S. AL from ALL (drops one L) + AY vowel team.
terms.exampleUsage: She ALWAYS helps her friends.

terms.definition: AL prefix + long O: AL + M + O + S + T. AL from ALL + O before ST says long /oh/.
terms.exampleUsage: We are ALMOST there!

terms.definition: Consonant-LE ending: C + R + U + M + B + LE. The -LE ending needs E as its syllable vowel (Rule 20).
terms.exampleUsage: The old wall started to CRUMBLE.

terms.definition: EA team + CH team + -ES: teach + es. Add -ES after CH (Rule 23). EA says /ee/.
terms.exampleUsage: She TEACHES math to young children.

terms.definition: AL prefix + compound: AL + TOGETHER. AL from ALL (drops one L, Rule 27).
terms.exampleUsage: There were ALTOGETHER fifty students.

terms.definition: Z at start + PH team: Z + E + PH + Y + R. Z says /z/. PH says /f/ (Greek origin).
terms.exampleUsage: A gentle ZEPHYR blew through the garden.

terms.definition: CH team + EW team + -ING: chew + ing. Two teams plus a suffix.
terms.exampleUsage: Stop CHEWING with your mouth open.

terms.definition: TH team + OUGH combo: TH + OUGH + T. OUGH says /aw/ here.
terms.exampleUsage: I THOUGHT about it all day.

terms.definition: OUGH combo: B + R + OUGH + T. OUGH says /aw/ — same sound as in THOUGHT.
terms.exampleUsage: She BROUGHT flowers to the party.

terms.definition: Consonant blend + consonant-LE: S + C + R + A + M + B + LE. -LE ending needs E (Rule 20).
terms.exampleUsage: Let's SCRAMBLE some eggs for breakfast.

terms.definition: IGH + -ER comparative: bright + er. IGH says /eye/. -ER makes it comparative.
terms.exampleUsage: Stars look BRIGHTER in the countryside.

terms.definition: TH + NK + -FUL: Multiple consonant teams + suffix. TH says /th/, NK says /ngk/.
terms.exampleUsage: Be THANKFUL for what you have.

terms.definition: SH team + Silent E: SH + I + N + E. SH says /sh/. Silent E makes I long /eye/.
terms.exampleUsage: Let your light SHINE!

terms.definition: CH team + OI team: CH + OI + C + E. Two teams working together. E keeps C soft.
terms.exampleUsage: Make a good CHOICE.

terms.definition: OW team + ER: T + OW + ER. OW says /ow/ here. ER says /er/.
terms.exampleUsage: Look at the tall TOWER!

terms.definition: OW says long O: S + N + OW. OW says /oh/ here — different from COW.
terms.exampleUsage: The SNOW covered the mountains.

terms.definition: TH team + EA team: TH + R + EA + D. TH says unvoiced /th/. EA says short /e/ here.
terms.exampleUsage: Use a needle and THREAD to sew.

terms.definition: WH team + EE team: WH + EE + L. Two teams in one word. EE says /ee/.
terms.exampleUsage: Turn the steering WHEEL.

terms.definition: CH at start and end: CH + UR + CH. Same consonant team appears twice. UR says /er/.
terms.exampleUsage: The old CHURCH has a beautiful bell.

terms.definition: TH team + silent B: TH + U + M + B. B is silent after M (Rule 33).
terms.exampleUsage: She pricked her THUMB on a thorn.

terms.definition: WR team + NG team: WR + O + NG. W is silent in WR. NG says /ng/.
terms.exampleUsage: That answer is WRONG.

terms.definition: KN team + EE team: KN + EE. K is silent in KN. EE says /ee/.
terms.exampleUsage: She hurt her KNEE playing sports.

terms.definition: KN team + OW team: KN + OW. K is silent. OW says /oh/ here.
terms.exampleUsage: I KNOW the answer!

terms.definition: GR blend + PH team: G + R + A + PH. PH says /f/ — Greek origin (Rule 38).
terms.exampleUsage: Draw a GRAPH to show the data.
